title: The Core of Calvinism, Part 2: Sola Gratia
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 7–14-24 - The Core of Calvinism 2 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Building on Lesson One
subpoints: Calvinism has been maligned in the world and sadly in the church
In history, the majority of Protestant Christians were Calvinists
Famous Calvinist missionaries and evangelists burned with evangelistic zeal because of their theology
The theme: Calvinism translates into daily life as living for God's glory with great joy
The five Reformation solas: Scripture alone, grace alone, faith alone, Christ alone, glory to God alone

outline.title: Defining Grace
subpoints: Hebrew and Greek terms point to kindness, favor, graciousness
Grace is something to be desired and wished upon others (Apostolic benedictions)
Grace comes from God alone, not from anything outside him
Grace especially comes through the Lord Jesus Christ
Grace is not a thing — it is God's favor, God's disposition toward people in Christ
Grace is unmerited favor — but more: it is unmerited favor in the place of merited wrath

outline.title: Grace in the New Testament: A Survey
subpoints: Grace is the means of justification (Titus 3:3-7)
Grace is the source of hope (2 Thessalonians 2:16)
Grace is the source of rejoicing (Colossians 3:16)
God's whole dealing with his people is called grace (Ephesians 1:7)
Grace equips men and women with gifts for service (Romans 12:6)
Grace is the basis of God's election (Romans 11:5-6)
Grace is communicated through the Word of God (Acts 20:32)

outline.title: Does the Bible Really Teach Sovereign Grace? Two Key Passages
subpoints: Ephesians 2:4-5: even when we were dead in sins, God made us alive — by grace you have been saved
Titus 3:3-7: we ourselves were once foolish — but when God's kindness appeared, he saved us through washing of regeneration
The unbeliever is not merely helpless and uninformed but is dead and in active rebellion against God
The mystery is not why some go to hell, but why God saves anyone

outline.title: Sovereign Grace in Romans
subpoints: Romans 4:16 — grace makes Jews and Gentiles heirs together with Abraham
Romans 5:1-2 — grace establishes peace between God and sinners who are his enemies
Romans 5:20-21 — grace is stronger than sin
Romans 6:14 — sin shall have no dominion over you, for you are not under law but under grace
Romans 11:5-6 — election is by grace, not by works; if by works, it would no longer be grace

outline.title: The Effects of Sovereign Grace
subpoints: Sovereign grace crushes pride — we want to be the subject, not the object of salvation
Sovereign grace creates dependence on God — every breath depends on the gracious act of God
Sovereign grace prevents grumbling — by the grace of God, we are who we are (1 Corinthians 15:10)

outline.title: Conclusion: Amazing Grace from the Heart
subpoints: Only the old-fashioned gospel of sovereign grace captures and transforms sinners by the Spirit's power
The great hymns of the faith require believing what they say to sing them truthfully
"Prone to wander, Lord I feel it — Here's my heart, O take and seal it" — this presupposes sovereign grace

summary: The second lesson in the "Core of Calvinism" series focuses on the second Reformation sola — grace alone (Sola Gratia). Pastor Tito Lyro begins by noting that while Calvinism has been maligned, it was historically the mainstream of Protestant Christianity, and that faithful Calvinist missionaries like Whitefield, Spurgeon, and William Carey were energized by their theology rather than paralyzed by it. The central question is: does man initiate and participate in his salvation, or does God alone initiate and complete it? The lesson surveys the New Testament use of "grace," defining it not as a mere thing but as God's own favorable disposition toward people in Christ — unmerited favor in the place of merited wrath. Two anchor texts, Ephesians 2:4-5 and Titus 3:3-7, demonstrate that the unregenerate person is not merely ignorant but actively dead in sin and in rebellion. A survey of Romans further shows that election is by grace alone, not by foreseen works. The lesson closes by identifying the effects of sovereign grace: it crushes pride, creates dependence on God, and prevents grumbling. The session ends with a challenge to sing the great hymns of the faith truthfully, as their theology is inseparable from the doctrine of sovereign grace.
transcript:
## Introduction: Building on Lesson One

We're continuing on the series that we started last Sunday — an eight-part series on the system of doctrine known as Calvinism. I know that some people don't like using the label Calvinism, and I don't usually do that when talking to people outside the church, but right here with us, it gives us just a quick way to refer to something.

One of the reasons for Calvinism's decline in reputation is the current popularity of Arminianism. Because of that, we tend to think that Calvinists or Reformed believers are always in the minority. But the reality is that in the history of the Protestant Church, the majority of professing Christians were Calvinists. Missionaries and evangelists such as George Whitefield and Charles Spurgeon, William Carey, David Brainerd, Jonathan Edwards, Asahel Nettleton, Adoniram Judson, John Paton, David Livingstone, Hudson Taylor, and many others were staunch Calvinists who, because of Calvinism, burned with evangelistic zeal for the gospel. It was because of their firm belief in the sovereignty of God that they actually were able to accomplish the things that they accomplished.

So I wanted us to be confident in our Reformed faith. There is more to Calvinism than the famous five points. What I really want us to see is that Calvinism translates into daily life as living for God's glory with great joy. We've seen from Scripture that everything is about God — Romans 11:36: "For of him and through him and to him are all things, to whom be glory forever."

This side of Calvinism is beautifully summarized in the very first question of the Heidelberg Catechism: "What is your only comfort in life and death? That I am not my own, but belong — with body and soul, both in life and in death — to my faithful Savior Jesus Christ."

The men of the Reformation defended five important doctrines: Scripture alone, grace alone, faith alone, Christ alone, and all glory to God alone. In light of what we saw last week — that the marrow of Calvinism is God's sovereignty — I want us to look at the second of these Reformation doctrines today: the salvation of God's elect is entirely due to God's grace, Sola Gratia.

## Defining Grace

The question that needs to be answered is: does man initiate and participate in his forgiveness and salvation, or does God initiate and complete the salvation of sinners so that the whole work is attributed to sovereign grace alone?

Let's define grace. In the Old Testament, the Hebrew term suggests kindness, favor, graciousness. In the New Testament, the Greek term charis means goodwill, loving kindness, favor — grace is something to be desired. You can see that in the several greetings and benedictions in the New Testament letters. Paul wishes grace upon people; John in Revelation 22:21 says, "The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all, amen."

Grace comes from God, not from anything else. James 4:6 says, "He gives more grace; therefore he says, God resists the proud but gives grace to the humble." Hebrews 4:16 says, "Let us come, therefore, boldly to the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need." And that grace comes especially through the Lord Jesus Christ. 2 Timothy 2:1 — "You therefore, may be strong in the grace that is in Christ Jesus." John 1:14 — the Word became flesh, full of grace and truth. John 1:17 — "For the law was given through Moses, but grace and truth came through Jesus Christ."

Grace is not merely unmerited favor — that is not sufficient when applied to sinful men in a redemptive context. It is unmerited favor in the place of merited wrath. Because as sinners, we have not merely failed to earn God's favor — we have actively merited his wrath. Grace is God's blessing freely bestowed on ill-deserving sinners. It is not a thing. God does not have a tank in heaven full of grace. Grace is God's favor upon people — God's disposition toward people in Christ. And that disposition never changes, because Christ never changes.

## Grace in the New Testament: A Survey

Titus 3:3-7 — grace is the means of justification: "For we ourselves were once foolish, disobedient, deceived...But when the kindness and love of God our Savior toward man appeared, not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to His mercy He saved us, through the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Spirit...having been justified by His grace we should become heirs according to the hope of eternal life."

Titus 2:11 — "For the grace of God that brings salvation has appeared." Ephesians 2:8 — "For by grace you have been saved through faith."

Grace is the source of hope: 2 Thessalonians 2:16. Grace is the source of rejoicing: Colossians 3:16. God's whole dealing with his people is called grace: Ephesians 1:7 — "In him we have redemption through his blood and forgiveness of sins according to the riches of his grace." Grace is glorious: Ephesians 1:6 — "to the praise of the glory of his grace."

Grace enables us to do all things: 2 Corinthians 9:8 — "And God is able to make all grace abound toward you, that you, always having all sufficiency in all things, may have an abundance for every good work." Grace transforms: 1 Corinthians 15:10 — "By the grace of God I am what I am."

Grace is communicated through the Word of God: Acts 20:32 — "And now, brethren, I commend you to God and to the word of His grace, which is able to build you up and give you an inheritance among all those who are sanctified."

## Does the Bible Really Teach Sovereign Grace?

Ephesians 2:4-5 — "But God, who is rich in mercy, because of his great love with which he loved us, even when we were dead in trespasses and sins, made us alive together with Christ — by grace you have been saved." Dead. Not merely sick. Not merely uninformed. Dead. And his death is against God, Paul compounds here: not just dead, but dead in trespasses and sins against God.

Titus 3:3-7 again — "For we ourselves were once foolish, disobedient, deceived, serving various lusts and pleasures, living in malice and envy, hateful and hating one another." This is talking about the people in the church — the first-person plural, "we were that." But then — "when the kindness and the love of God our Savior toward man appeared, not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to his mercy, he saved us."

Those who receive grace are not merely helpless sinners who are undeserving, but hostile rebels against God with bad hearts and bad records. The mystery in the scriptures is not why God is going to have to help the mystery in the scriptures is why God is saving anyone — when he could be loving, just, fair, holy, and all the things we learn from him, and still let every last person go to hell.

## Sovereign Grace in Romans

Romans 4:16 — "Therefore it is of faith that it might be according to grace, so that the promise might be sure to all the seed, not only to those who are of the law, but also to those who are of the faith of Abraham." Grace sovereignly brings Jews and Gentiles as members of the same body.

Romans 5:1-2 — "Therefore, having been justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ, through whom also we have access by faith into this grace in which we stand." Grace establishes peace with God.

Romans 5:20-21 — "Where sin abounded, grace abounded much more, so that sin reigned in death, even so grace might reign through righteousness to eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord." There is only one thing stronger than sin in us, and it is the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ.

Romans 6:14 — "For sin shall not have dominion over you, for you are not under law but under grace."

Romans 11:5-6 — "Even so then, at this present time there is a remnant according to the election of grace. And if by grace, then it is no longer of works; otherwise grace is no longer grace." This verse must put to rest any idea that God saves people because, in the course of time, he foresaw them obeying him and therefore chose to save them. If that were how it worked, Paul says, it would not be grace — it would be works. Election is completely by grace and grace alone.

## The Effects of Sovereign Grace

We don't like the effects of sovereign grace very much because the first thing it does is crush pride. We want to be the subject, not the object of salvation. We want to be the doer of our salvation, not the one upon whom salvation is done. Sovereign grace says, no, you have nothing to do with it. Stop being so proud. Stop looking at yourself. Look to Christ. And we don't like that because it crushes pride.

The second effect: sovereign grace creates dependence on God. If we are convinced that the next breath we take depends on the gracious act of God, we will look to him for all things. Life must come from God before the sinner can arise from the grave. We always want to be in charge, to determine our own lives. And every time we do that, we spiral down. The only way to spiral up is to realize that it is the grace of God, manifested to sinners who don't deserve it.

## Conclusion: Amazing Grace from the Heart

This free sovereign grace cries out for expressing in today's church. Human decisions, crowd manipulation, and altar calls are not where the power of God is. Only the old-fashioned gospel of sovereign grace will capture and transform sinners by the power of the Word of God and the Spirit of God.

Let me end with a stanza from the hymn that was chosen this morning: "O to grace, how great a debtor daily I'm constrained to be! Let that grace now like a fetter bind my wandering heart to thee. Prone to wander, Lord, I feel it — prone to leave the God I love. Here's my heart, O take and seal it, seal it for the courts above."

If you don't believe in the sovereign grace of God, when you sing that, you're lying. Can you sing the great hymns of the faith truthfully, knowing and believing what the Bible says concerning the grace of God?

Let's pray together. Father, thank you that you're good, that you're gracious, and that through the Lord Jesus Christ, you show that to us. We pray that you prepare us to come before you in worship this morning, that you might be glorified in our midst. In Jesus' name, amen.
